title: Kotlin Named Arguments
content:
### Named Arguments in Kotlin

Named arguments allow you to specify which parameter an argument belongs to by name, improving code readability and flexibility.

---

### Basic Named Arguments

```kotlin
fun createUser(username: String, email: String, age: Int) {
    println("$username ($age) - $email")
}

// Using named arguments
createUser(
    username = "alice",
    email = "alice@example.com",
    age = 30
)
```

---

### Mixing Positional and Named Arguments

Positional arguments must come before named arguments.

```kotlin
fun describe(name: String, age: Int, city: String) {
    println("$name, $age, $city")
}

describe("Alice", city = "New York", age = 30)  // OK
describe(name = "Alice", 30, "New York")  // Error: positional after named
```

---

### Skipping Parameters

Named arguments allow skipping optional parameters with defaults.

```kotlin
fun configure(
    host: String,
    port: Int = 8080,
    ssl: Boolean = false,
    timeout: Int = 5000
) {
    // ...
}

// Skip port, provide host and ssl
configure(host = "localhost", ssl = true)
```

---

### Improving Readability

Named arguments make boolean parameters self-documenting.

```kotlin
// Without named arguments -- unclear
updateUser("alice", true, false, true)

// With named arguments -- clear
updateUser(
    username = "alice",
    isActive = true,
    isAdmin = false,
    sendEmail = true
)
```

---

### Named Arguments with Vararg

```kotlin
fun printNumbers(vararg numbers: Int, prefix: String) {
    println("$prefix: ${numbers.joinToString(", ")}")
}

printNumbers(1, 2, 3, prefix = "Values")
```

> **Key Point:** Use named arguments to make function calls self-documenting, especially for boolean parameters and functions with many parameters.

quiz.question: Can positional arguments come after named arguments?
What is a benefit of named arguments?
